If most of the footprint can be resolved but a small bounded endpoint area remains ambiguous because the cursor or ongoing motion still obscures it, Stage 3 may allow the union to survive rather than forcing a false rejection.
This is acceptable because Stage 3 is a coarse screening stage and later stages are responsible for finer local temporal analysis.

However, Stage 3 should still reject clearly weak unions whenever the available evidence is sufficient to invalidate the footprint, including any leftover ambiguous cells that can be invalidated by trustworthy after-reference checks.
Endpoint ambiguity cap rule
Endpoint ambiguity is allowed only when the remaining resolved ambiguous area is small, localized, and not already contradicted by the resolved evidence.

A candidate union must not survive on endpoint ambiguity if the resolved ambiguous cells are too numerous, too spatially diffuse, or too disconnected from the endpoint-style obscuration that created the ambiguity.
For Stage 3, endpoint ambiguity should be treated as bounded only when all of the following are true:

text:list-item.text:p: the resolved ambiguous cells occupy no more than a small minority of the full union footprint
the resolved ambiguous cells remain spatially localized as one connected ambiguous cluster rather than being scattered broadly across the footprint
the resolved ambiguous cells are plausibly explained by endpoint-style obscuration, lingering cursor coverage, or similarly localized unresolved contamination
the resolved changed and resolved unchanged evidence outside that ambiguous remainder does not already justify clear rejection

office:text.text:p: If the resolved ambiguous area grows too large, too diffuse, or too weakly tied to endpoint-style obscuration, it should no longer qualify as bounded ambiguity.
In those cases, Stage 3 should decide the union from the resolved evidence that is available, rather than allowing ambiguity itself to keep the union alive.
Bounded-ambiguity spatial rule
For the first implementation, bounded ambiguity should satisfy a simple connected-cluster rule in addition to the existing size and endpoint-style rules.
A resolved ambiguous remainder should count as spatially bounded only when it forms one connected ambiguous cluster within the footprint.
For the current default design, ambiguous cells belong to the same ambiguous cluster only when they are orthogonally adjacent to one another within the footprint.
Diagonal-only contact does not count as belonging to the same ambiguous cluster for this bounded-ambiguity rule.

If the remaining resolved ambiguous cells are split into multiple separated clusters, Stage 3 should not treat that remainder as bounded ambiguity unless a later tuning revision explicitly allows a more permissive rule.
Design note:
This rule exists to make the bounded-ambiguity shape constraint operational for implementation.

For the first implementation, Stage 3 should treat one small connected ambiguous cluster as eligible for bounded-ambiguity survival, but should not treat multiple separated ambiguous islands as equivalent to one localized endpoint remainder.
Rejection-priority rule
Endpoint ambiguity must not override strong resolved-unchanged evidence.

If the resolved footprint evidence already shows that the union has no meaningful lasting change and the remaining ambiguous cells are too limited, too weak, or too unsupported to plausibly change that conclusion, the union should be rejected.
Tuning note
The exact numeric cap for maximum allowed resolved ambiguous cells, and the exact spatial rule for how localized they must remain, are tuning parameters rather than conceptual truths.
These values should be defined separately as Stage 3 tuning parameters.
Design note:
This rule keeps endpoint ambiguity narrow.
Endpoint ambiguity exists to prevent false rejection when a small leftover area cannot be confidently invalidated at Stage 3's coarse level.
It does not exist to let large or diffuse unresolved regions survive merely because Stage 3 could not finish resolving them.
Comparison region rule

Stage 3 should compare reference material using the union footprint or the relevant unresolved sub-region of that footprint. It should not compare the whole frame, and it should not yet use Stage 4 or Stage 5 time-slice logic.
Lasting change rule

For Stage 3, enough lasting change means that the union footprint or a meaningful portion of it shows a visible difference from the reconstructed before state, and that this difference remains present after the movement has passed through that region strongly enough to count as a persistent result rather than a transient cursor or overlay artifact.
Stage 3 should not require precision about exactly where within the union the final valid change occurred. It only needs enough evidence to decide whether the union is worth refining further.
Stage 3 classification rule
A candidate union should be rejected when Stage 3 can confidently determine that the union footprint shows no meaningful lasting change.

A candidate union should survive when Stage 3 can confidently determine that meaningful lasting change is present, or when a bounded resolved ambiguous portion of the footprint cannot be confidently invalidated at Stage 3's coarse level.

A candidate union should not survive merely because the comparison is weak, noisy, or inconvenient. Survival should remain tied to either positive evidence of lasting change or unresolved ambiguity that cannot be confidently invalidated.
Debug output rule
Stage 3 should record enough debug output to make the union-screening decision auditable.
At minimum, Stage 3 debug output should record:

text:list-item.text:p: which Stage 3 path was used for the union
whether the simple nearby comparison path was sufficient
whether snapshot rescue was used
whether the union survived with endpoint ambiguity
whether a trustworthy whole-footprint before reference was found for Step 1
whether a trustworthy whole-footprint after reference was found for Step 1
whether any cell-specific after-reference invalidation was used
whether later material was considered and rejected under the anti-borrowing rule

office:text.text:p: Stage 3 should also record footprint-resolution coverage values that are sufficient to explain the final decision.
At minimum, this should include values equivalent to:

text:list-item.text:p: resolved_changed_coverage
resolved_unchanged_coverage
resolved_ambiguous_coverage

office:text.text:p: If useful for tuning and investigation, Stage 3 may also record:

text:list-item.text:p: step1_before_reference_available
step1_after_reference_available
snapshot_rescue_used
surviving_with_endpoint_ambiguity
cell_specific_after_invalidation_used
anti_borrowing_guardrail_triggered
final_stage3_outcome_reason

office:text.text:p: Example outcome reasons may include:

text:list-item.text:p: survived_by_changed_evidence
survived_by_bounded_ambiguity
rejected_by_resolved_unchanged_evidence
rejected_after_rescue_failure
step1_clear_survival
step1_clear_rejection

office:text.text:p: Design note:
These debug outputs are not part of the conceptual validity decision itself.

Their purpose is to make Stage 3 behavior inspectable during implementation, tuning, and benchmark review, especially in cases involving snapshot rescue, endpoint ambiguity, or possible borrowed-future contamination.
Stage 3 terminology rule
For Stage 3, the following terms should be used consistently:
clean / contaminated
These terms describe whether a footprint cell or reference window is free of meaningful movement contamination.
A clean cell or clean reference window is one that is usable for coarse comparison.
A contaminated cell or contaminated reference window is one that is still affected by meaningful movement, obscuration, or unstable comparison state.
trustworthy
This term describes whether a reference window is acceptable for Stage 3 use.
A reference window is trustworthy only when the evaluated footprint cells within it are clean enough and stable enough to support the intended comparison.
settled / unsettled
These terms describe whether a post-movement visual state has stabilized enough to support lasting-change judgment.
A settled region may support coarse or local comparison.
An unsettled region must not be treated as lasting-change support yet.
resolved changed / resolved unchanged / resolved ambiguous
These are the official Stage 3 footprint-coverage buckets. Stage 3 should use these terms rather than looser alternatives when describing the outcome of cell-level comparison.
reference window
This term should be used for the before reference window and after reference window used by Stage 3.
The document should prefer reference window over bare window when the meaning is specifically the Stage 3 comparison window.
full footprint
This term should be used when Stage 3 means the entire union footprint.
sub-region or cell-specific region should be used when only part of the footprint is being evaluated.
bounded ambiguity / endpoint ambiguity
Endpoint ambiguity is the specific Stage 3 case where a small localized unresolved remainder remains near the endpoint of the union because of lingering obscuration or unresolved contamination.
Bounded ambiguity is the broader rule category that allows survival only when that unresolved remainder stays small, localized, and not contradicted by the resolved evidence.
Design note:
This terminology rule exists to keep Stage 3 implementation and debugging language consistent.

The document should avoid switching casually between similar words such as usable, quiet, clean, trustworthy, unresolved, and ambiguous when one of the standardized terms above is the more precise choice.
Stage 3 coverage-bucket rule
For Stage 3, the union footprint should be evaluated at the level of individual footprint cells.
After Step 1 and, if needed, snapshot rescue, each footprint cell should be placed into one of three coverage buckets:

text:list-item.text:p: resolved changed
resolved unchanged
resolved ambiguous

office:text.text:p: A cell is resolved changed when Stage 3 has enough trustworthy comparison evidence to determine that the cell shows lasting change relative to the reconstructed before state.
A cell is resolved unchanged when Stage 3 has enough trustworthy comparison evidence to determine that the cell does not show lasting change relative to the reconstructed before state.
A cell is resolved ambiguous when Stage 3 does not yet have enough trustworthy comparison evidence to classify the cell as either changed or unchanged.

The purpose of these buckets is to make the Stage 3 decision explicit. Stage 3 does not decide union survival from vague overall impressions. It decides from how much of the footprint has been resolved as changed, resolved as unchanged, or remains resolved ambiguous.
Stage 3 coverage aggregation rule
For the current default design, Stage 3 coverage values should be computed as the proportion of union-footprint cells assigned to each Stage 3 coverage bucket.
This means:

text:list-item.text:p: resolved changed coverage = the number of union-footprint cells classified as resolved changed divided by the total number of cells in the union footprint
resolved unchanged coverage = the number of union-footprint cells classified as resolved unchanged divided by the total number of cells in the union footprint
resolved ambiguous coverage = the number of union-footprint cells classified as resolved ambiguous divided by the total number of cells in the union footprint

office:text.text:p: These coverage values should therefore be normalized proportions in the range 0.0 to 1.0.
Coverage aggregation note
For the current default design, Stage 3 should use unweighted footprint-cell coverage.
Each footprint cell contributes equally to the Stage 3 coverage totals unless a later design revision explicitly introduces weighted coverage.
This means Stage 3 should not assume confidence weighting, area weighting, or connectedness weighting unless those rules are later added explicitly.
Design note:
This aggregation rule exists to ensure that Stage 3 placeholder thresholds, debug outputs, and union-level decision rules all refer to the same underlying coverage measurement.
Stage 3 changed-cluster rule
For the current default design, the minimum changed-cluster size for changed-evidence survival should be interpreted spatially rather than as any two changed cells anywhere in the footprint.
A changed cluster means one connected group of footprint cells that are all classified as resolved changed.
For the current placeholder rule, cells belong to the same changed cluster only when they are orthogonally adjacent to one another within the footprint.
Diagonal-only contact does not count as belonging to the same changed cluster for this minimum-cluster rule.
This means the placeholder parameter:

text:list-item.text:p: Minimum Changed-Cluster Size For Survival = 2 cells

office:text.text:p: should be interpreted as requiring at least one connected resolved-changed cluster containing two orthogonally adjacent footprint cells before Stage 3 may treat changed evidence as strong enough for changed-evidence survival.
Design note:
This rule exists to prevent Stage 3 from treating two scattered single changed cells as equivalent to one small coherent changed patch.
The exact connectivity rule may be tuned later, but the first implementation should use this simple connected-cluster interpretation.
Stage 3 union-level decision rule
Stage 3 should make its union-level survive or reject decision from the footprint coverage buckets.
A candidate union should survive when enough footprint cells are resolved changed to show meaningful lasting change.
A candidate union should also survive when a bounded remainder of the footprint remains resolved ambiguous and that ambiguous remainder cannot be confidently invalidated at Stage 3's coarse level.

A candidate union should be rejected when the resolved footprint evidence shows no meaningful lasting change and any remaining ambiguous cells can either be invalidated by trustworthy after-reference checks or are too weak or too insignificant to prevent rejection.
A candidate union must not survive merely because the comparison is noisy, weak, inconvenient, or incomplete in a generic sense.
Ambiguity-based survival applies only when the ambiguous remainder is real, bounded, and cannot be confidently invalidated by the evidence available to Stage 3.
This rule makes Stage 3 concrete without turning it into a slice-level refinement stage.

Stage 3 still remains a coarse whole-union screening pass. It may reason about individual footprint cells in order to decide whether the union as a whole should survive, but it does not use those cell decisions to temporally localize exact valid internal ranges. That remains the responsibility of Stage 4 and Stage 5.
Stage 3 placeholder threshold rule
For implementation and early benchmark testing, Stage 3 should use explicit placeholder thresholds when converting footprint coverage buckets into survive or reject decisions.
The purpose of these thresholds is not to define final truth, but to prevent implementation drift while the design is being tested and tuned.
The following placeholder thresholds should be used for the current default design:

text:list-item.text:p: Minimum Resolved-Changed Coverage For Survival = 0.10
Maximum Resolved-Ambiguous Coverage For Bounded Ambiguity = 0.10
Minimum Reconstructed-Before Coverage For Rescue-Supported Survival = 0.80
Resolved-Unchanged Rejection Priority Threshold = 0.85
Minimum Changed-Cluster Size For Survival = 2 cells

office:text.text:p: Threshold precedence rule
If more than one threshold-based outcome appears to be satisfied at the same time, Stage 3 must resolve that conflict by precedence rather than by ad hoc judgment.
For the current default design, rejection-priority takes precedence over changed-evidence survival when the resolved-unchanged rejection-priority threshold is met.
This means a candidate union must not survive by changed-evidence threshold alone if the same footprint evidence also satisfies the rejection-priority rule.
Changed-evidence survival should be evaluated only after the rejection-priority rule has failed.
Threshold interpretation rule
A candidate union should survive by changed evidence only when all of the following are true:

text:list-item.text:p: resolved changed coverage reaches at least the minimum resolved-changed coverage threshold
the changed evidence includes at least one connected resolved-changed cluster that satisfies the minimum changed-cluster size under the Stage 3 changed-cluster rule
the resolved-unchanged rejection-priority threshold is not simultaneously satisfied

office:text.text:p: A candidate union may survive by bounded ambiguity only when resolved ambiguous coverage does not exceed the maximum bounded-ambiguity threshold and the other bounded-ambiguity rules are satisfied.

A candidate union should not reach rescue-supported survival through either full-resolution rescue or partial-resolution changed-evidence survival unless the reconstructed before-state coverage reaches at least the minimum reconstructed-before coverage threshold.

A candidate union may be rejected by resolved evidence when resolved unchanged coverage reaches at least the rejection-priority threshold and the remaining resolved ambiguous coverage does not exceed the bounded-ambiguity threshold or has otherwise been confidently invalidated.
Design note:
These are temporary implementation thresholds for the current default design.
They should be treated as starting values for testing, debugging, and benchmark review, not as permanent design truths.
Stage 3 / Stage 4 boundary rule
Stage 3 and Stage 4 serve different purposes and must remain distinct.
Stage 3 is a coarse whole-union screening stage.

Stage 3 is allowed to inspect the union footprint spatially, reason about individual footprint cells, reconstruct a composite before-state baseline, use trustworthy cell-specific invalidation checks, and determine whether the union as a whole should survive or be rejected.

However, Stage 3 must not divide the union into internal temporal units, must not assign validity to exact internal time ranges, and must not attempt to label which specific sub-ranges inside the union are the true valid portions.
Stage 4 is the first stage that performs explicit internal temporal localization.
Once a union survives Stage 3, Stage 4 may divide that surviving union into time slices and classify those internal temporal parts as valid, invalid, or undetermined.
Stage 3 may use the union's own later internal settled state only as coarse support for the union-level survival decision.
It may not use that internal inspection to define exact valid start and end points inside the union.
Design note:
The purpose of this boundary is to let Stage 3 become smarter about reference validity and coarse rescue logic without turning it into a hidden time-slicing stage.
Stage 3 decides whether the union is worth refining.
Stage 4 begins the work of determining where inside that surviving union the valid temporal material actually lies.
Design principles for Stage 3

text:list-item.text:p: use the union footprint, not the whole frame
prefer nearby trustworthy references first
use short reference windows for the simple path, not single frames
allow composite before-state rescue when a single clean reference is unavailable
avoid borrowing unrelated future activity as proof for the current union
treat lasting change as something that remains after movement has passed through the region
optimize for coarse screening, not precision slicing
reject clearly weak unions early while keeping genuinely plausible or unresolved unions alive for refinement
allow a broad mixed union to survive when it contains one or more locally credible settled-change clusters
do not require valid lasting change to dominate the entire union footprint in order for the union to survive
derive local clusters from the union’s own movement footprint rather than from arbitrary fixed canvas partitions

office:text.text:p: Placeholder tuning guidance
The exact numeric parameters for Stage 3 are still tuning parameters, not final design truths.
These include:

text:list-item.text:p: the nearby simple-search window length
the before reference window length
the after reference window length
the minimum evidence needed for clear broad union-level survival
the minimum credible local cluster footprint
the local cluster merge adjacency rule
the minimum local persistent-difference score for a credible changed cluster
the minimum local after-window persistence for a credible changed cluster
the maximum unresolved ratio allowed inside a credible changed cluster

office:text.text:p: These values should be defined separately as tunable parameters, not embedded into the conceptual definition itself.
Initial default parameters
For initial implementation and benchmark testing, Stage 3 should initially assume:

text:list-item.text:p: Nearby Simple Search Window = 00:00:10:00
Before Reference Window Length = 00:00:00:10
Before Reference Offset = 00:00:00:05
After Reference Delay = 00:00:00:05
After Reference Window Length = 00:00:00:10
Maximum Snapshot Rescue Search Distance = 00:00:30:00
Stage 3 Reference Stability Check Length = 3 frames
Stage 3 Internal Settlement Check Length = 3 frames
Minimum Resolved-Changed Coverage For Survival = 0.10
Maximum Resolved-Ambiguous Coverage For Bounded Ambiguity = 0.10
Minimum Reconstructed-Before Coverage For Rescue-Supported Survival = 0.80
Resolved-Unchanged Rejection Priority Threshold = 0.85
Minimum Changed-Cluster Size For Survival = 2 cells

office:text.text:p: These are starting defaults, not final truths, and may be tuned later through benchmark testing.
Open parameter questions
The exact placeholder values still need to be chosen for:

text:list-item.text:p: the nearby simple-search window length
the before reference window length
the after reference window length
the broad union-level survival threshold
the minimum credible local cluster footprint
the local cluster merge adjacency rule
the local cluster valid threshold
the maximum unresolved ratio allowed inside a rescuing cluster

office:text.text:p: These are tuning parameters, not conceptual definitions.
Stage 4: Probe-Based Band Detection
Purpose
Stage 4 takes each surviving union from Stage 3 and determines where in time the valid art-changing activity actually lives inside that union.
Its role is not to decide whether the union should survive at all. Stage 3 has already answered that coarse whole-union question.
Its role is to scan through the surviving union, detect where meaningful lasting canvas change begins and ends, and construct provisional valid activity bands from those findings.
Stage 4 should therefore operate as a temporal localization stage built on cell-level evidence rather than as a broad whole-slice scoring stage.
Inputs

text:list-item.text:p: surviving unions from Stage 3
the movement evidence record
reusable sampled frame payloads and art-state data from earlier stages
the baseline comparison state available for the surviving union
Stage 3 debug and support information when useful for local interpretation

The baseline comparison state is the best trusted “before” reference currently available for the cells being evaluated. It normally begins with the union-level before-state established in Stage 3, but Stage 4 may later supplement it with evidence gathered from earlier probes when that helps clarify local time structure.

office:text.text:p: Outputs
Stage 4 outputs a list of provisional time bands inside each surviving union. Each provisional band stores:

text:list-item.text:p: start_time
end_time
parent_union_index
classification
supporting probe evidence
boundary confidence metadata

office:text.text:p: Stage 4 may also output non-valid gaps between valid bands when that information is useful for later refinement or debugging.
Responsibilities

text:list-item.text:p: scans through each surviving union in time order
evaluates probe points across the union timeline
determines which currently relevant cells show meaningful lasting change
distinguishes changed, unchanged, contaminated, and unresolved local evidence
builds probe-level support over time
groups consecutive probe results into provisional valid activity bands
closes bands when valid support clearly ceases
passes uncertain band boundaries forward for later refinement
decide whether a union survives or is rejected
rely on broad whole-slice evidence as its primary decision unit
recursively subdivide time ranges by default in order to discover structure
finalize editorial clip padding or final output construction

office:text.text:p: Decision Model
Stage 4 uses a probe-based temporal localization model.

Rather than first splitting a surviving union into broad halves and asking whether each half is valid, Stage 4 scans the union from beginning to end using probe points placed at regular coarse intervals.

Each probe examines a short probe-local evaluation window around its probe anchor time, identifies the cells most relevant to that moment, and estimates whether meaningful lasting canvas change is present, absent, or still unresolved.
Stage 4 then uses the sequence of probe results to build provisional valid activity bands across the union timeline.
This means Stage 4 is primarily answering:

text:list-item.text:p: where in time valid art-changing activity begins
where it remains active
where it stops
whether internal gaps exist between separate valid activity bands

office:text.text:p: If the probe sequence already defines those bands clearly enough, Stage 4 should output them directly without unnecessary recursive subdivision.
Probe Model
A Stage 4 probe is a local time-based evaluation performed at a probe anchor time inside a surviving union.
The probe anchor time is the central timestamp the probe is organized around.
The probe-local evaluation window is the short local span around that anchor time used to gather movement, contamination, and cell-state evidence. It is not a single exact frame.
For each probe, Stage 4 should:

text:list-item.text:p: identify recently active cells inside the surviving union footprint
identify which of those cells are judgeable at the current probe
identify which relevant cells remain contaminated or unresolved
compare judgeable cells against the current baseline comparison state
determine whether the probe shows meaningful valid art-changing support

office:text.text:p: The probe is therefore the basic evidence unit Stage 4 uses to map valid activity through time.
Probe Result Fields
Each Stage 4 probe result should store:

text:list-item.text:p: probe_anchor_time: The central timestamp the probe is built around.
Probe_local_evaluation_window: The short local time span used to gather evidence for the probe.
Recently_active_cells: Cells with movement evidence during the probe-local window or in the span since the previous probe.
Judgeable_cells: Relevant cells that are currently readable enough to compare against the baseline comparison state.
contaminated_cells: Relevant cells that are still too obscured by current cursor presence or motion to support a clean decision.
confirmed_changed_cells: Judgeable cells that clearly show meaningful lasting change.
confirmed_unchanged_cells: Judgeable cells that remain within the unchanged / noise band.
unresolved_cells: Relevant cells that cannot yet be confidently called changed or unchanged.
changed_support_score: A probe-level score describing how much credible changed evidence is present.
unchanged_support_score: A probe-level score describing how much credible unchanged evidence is present.
unresolved_support_score: A probe-level score describing how much relevant evidence remains unresolved.
final_probe_label: The probe’s final classification after all local evidence has been summarized.

office:text.text:p: The recommended initial final probe labels are:

text:list-item.text:p: positive
negative
holding
unclear

office:text.text:p: Cell-level Judgment Rule
Stage 4 should judge cells individually only after first limiting attention to cells that are relevant to the current probe.
A judgeable cell should then be classified into one of three categories:

text:list-item.text:p: - confirmed_changed
- confirmed_unchanged
- unresolved

office:text.text:p: A judgeable cell becomes confirmed_changed when its current readable state differs from the baseline comparison state by enough to clearly exceed the normal flicker and noise level for that cell, and when that difference is consistent with meaningful lasting canvas change rather than transient contamination.
A judgeable cell becomes confirmed_unchanged when its current readable state remains within the normal no-change / ambient-noise band for that cell.
A judgeable cell becomes unresolved when the measured difference is too large to confidently call unchanged but not yet strong or stable enough to confidently call changed.
This means Stage 4 should use a three-band rule:

text:list-item.text:p: unchanged band near natural noise
changed band clearly above natural noise
unresolved band between them

office:text.text:p: The exact numeric thresholds are tuning parameters, but the changed threshold should be clearly above the normal flicker band so that small real art updates still clear noise without requiring large strokes.
Stage 4 Band Construction Rule
Stage 4 should construct provisional valid activity bands from the sequence of probe results across the surviving union.
A positive probe indicates that meaningful valid art-changing activity is present at that point in time.
A negative probe indicates that meaningful valid art-changing activity is not present at that point in time.

A holding probe indicates that the current area is still partially unresolved in a way that is consistent with valid activity continuing, such as when important cells remain contaminated by ongoing motion but surrounding evidence remains supportive.
An unclear probe indicates that the probe does not yet contribute enough reliable evidence to confidently help open or close a band.

Stage 4 should open, continue, or close provisional valid bands by examining the continuity of positive, holding, and negative probe results over time rather than by recursively halving the union by default.
Stage 4 Boundary Refinement Handoff rule
Stage 4 should aim to discover the correct internal valid bands of each surviving union at a coarse but structurally correct level.

If a provisional band’s boundaries remain uncertain after the coarse probe scan, Stage 4 should pass those uncertain boundaries forward for later refinement rather than recursively re-solving the entire interior of the band.
This means later refinement should focus mainly on sharpening starts and ends, not on rediscovering where valid activity exists at all.
Design principles for Stage 4

text:list-item.text:p: Stage 4 performs temporal localization, not union survival screening
it should scan through each surviving union in time order
it should use probe-based evidence rather than broad whole-slice scoring as its primary logic
it should reuse trusted earlier-stage comparison state whenever practical
it should judge only the cells that are relevant to the current probe
it should treat contamination as unresolved evidence, not automatically as negative evidence
it should allow unresolved evidence to be carried forward to later probes
it should discover valid activity bands directly when possible rather than relying on recursive halving by default
it should prefer structural correctness of valid bands over premature temporal precision
it should hand uncertain boundaries forward for refinement rather than forcing brittle decisions too early

office:text.text:p: Stage 4 Operational Rule Set
Probe Placement Rule
Stage 4 probes are placed at fixed intervals across each surviving union.
The first regular probe anchor should be placed 2 seconds after the start of the surviving union.
Each following regular probe anchor should be placed 2 seconds after the previous regular probe anchor.

Each probe uses a probe-local evaluation window centered on the probe anchor. The initial default probe-local evaluation window is 10 frames total: 5 frames before the probe anchor and 5 frames after the probe anchor.

If a surviving union is shorter than the normal 2-second probe interval, Stage 4 should not create a midpoint regular probe by default. In this case, Stage 4 should use the inherited Stage 3 before-state as the starting comparison state and should create a terminal probe near the end of the union.
This means very short surviving unions are still checked explicitly at their end without forcing an unnecessary midpoint probe.

Stage 4 should always create a terminal probe near the end of the surviving union. The initial default terminal probe anchor should be placed at union_end minus 5 frames, so that its 10-frame local window can inspect the final local state without extending beyond the union end.

If the final regular probe anchor and the terminal probe anchor would be too close together, Stage 4 may merge them into a single terminal-aware probe. The initial default minimum distance between separate probe anchors should be 1 second. If the final regular probe would fall within 1 second of the terminal probe, only the terminal probe should be kept.
The terminal probe exists to make sure the end of the union is explicitly checked, rather than relying only on the last regular 2-second probe.

Stage 3 after-reference information may still be retained for diagnostics or fallback comparison support, but it should not replace the Stage 4 terminal probe because the terminal probe is responsible for checking end-of-union local temporal structure rather than broad union-level survival evidence.
Each Stage 4 probe should evaluate a defined relevant cell set rather than treating the whole surviving union footprint as equally relevant at all times.
For the current default design, the relevant cell set for a probe should be the union of:
- cells touched by movement evidence inside the current probe-local evaluation window
- cells touched by movement evidence after the previous probe and before the current probe
- cells that remain part of an unresolved chain carried forward from earlier probes
- cells that were recently resolved changed and may still need baseline-update handling before they fully settle into the current known comparison state

This means the relevant cell set is not limited only to cells with fresh movement in the current probe window. It may also include older unresolved cells whose status still matters for band continuity, late resolution, or backfill decisions.
A cell should leave the relevant cell set when:
- it is no longer part of an unresolved chain
- it is no longer needed for recent changed-state settlement tracking
- and no fresh movement evidence has touched it in the current or immediately preceding probe interval
Negative, holding, and unclear decisions should be made against this relevant cell set, not against the full surviving union footprint.
This keeps Stage 4 focused on the cells that are temporally active, recently unsettled, or still awaiting resolution, rather than diluting probe decisions across stale inactive footprint regions.
Cell State Layer
The cell state layer describes what Stage 4 currently believes about individual cells inside the surviving union footprint.

Stage 4 should evaluate cells relative to the current known comparison state for that cell. At the beginning of a surviving union, this comparison state is inherited from the Stage 3 before-state for that union. As Stage 4 progresses, the comparison state may be updated when later probes establish a newer trusted state for a cell.
A Stage 4 cell may have one of the following local evaluation states:
confirmed_changed:

The cell is judgeable, and its current readable state differs from the current known comparison state by enough to clearly exceed normal flicker/noise. This is positive evidence that meaningful lasting canvas change has occurred.
confirmed_unchanged:

The cell is judgeable, and its current readable state remains within the normal no-change / ambient-noise band relative to the current known comparison state. This is evidence that no meaningful lasting canvas change has occurred in that cell since the current known comparison state.
contaminated:

The cell is currently believed to be obscured by a parked, hovering, or barely moving cursor/active visual object. A contaminated cell cannot be trusted as changed or unchanged because the visible contents of the cell may include transient cursor material rather than the settled canvas state. For the current default design, contamination is determined from the movement evidence record and cursor-location inference, not from the cell’s visual difference alone.
unresolved:

The cell is relevant to the current probe but cannot yet be confidently classified as confirmed_changed or confirmed_unchanged. This may happen because movement is actively passing through the cell, because the cell is contaminated, or because its measured difference sits in the uncertain band between normal noise and confirmed lasting change.
A contaminated cell is one specific kind of unresolved cell.

A cell may also be unresolved without being contaminated. This happens when the cell is not believed to contain a parked cursor, but ongoing movement or uncertain visual evidence still prevents a confident changed/unchanged judgment.
Contaminated is therefore a specific reason a cell may be unresolved. Unresolved is the broader carried-forward investigation state.
Cell Lifecycle Rule
Stage 4 should maintain a per-cell lifecycle record for cells that become relevant during a surviving union.
A cell lifecycle record may include:
inherited_state:
The trusted starting comparison state inherited from Stage 3 for this cell, when available. This is the first known state Stage 4 uses when evaluating the cell inside the surviving union.
current_known_state:

The most recent trusted settled state for the cell. At the start of Stage 4, this normally matches the inherited_state. If a later probe confirms that the cell has changed and that changed state is readable as settled canvas state, current_known_state may update to that newer state.
current_known_state_origin:
Metadata describing where the current_known_state came from. This should identify the source in a direct human-readable way, such as:
- inherited_stage3_before
- stage4_probe_3
- stage5_refinement
- inherited_stage3_before_union_2
current_known_state_established_at_probe:

The Stage 4 probe index where the current_known_state was last established or refreshed. If the state was inherited from Stage 3 before Stage 4 began, this should be marked as inherited or not_applicable rather than forcing a numeric probe index.
unresolved_since_probe:
The first probe index where this cell became unresolved in its current unresolved chain. If the cell is no longer unresolved, this should be empty or not applicable.
last_seen_probe:
The latest probe index where this cell was relevant to Stage 4 evaluation, whether as changed, unchanged, contaminated, or unresolved.
last_resolved_probe:

The latest probe index where this cell was confidently classified as confirmed_changed or confirmed_unchanged. If the cell has never been resolved during Stage 4, this should be empty or not applicable.
unresolved_reason:
The reason the cell is currently unresolved. Examples include active_movement, suspected_cursor_contamination, missing_trustworthy_reference, or uncertain_difference_band.
A cell’s unresolved_since_probe should reset when the cell becomes confidently resolved as confirmed_changed or confirmed_unchanged.
If the same cell later becomes unresolved again after being resolved, that later unresolved period should begin a new unresolved chain with a new unresolved_since_probe.
Contamination and Judgeability Rule
Stage 4 should use movement evidence to determine whether a relevant cell is currently judgeable.
A cell is judgeable when Stage 4 has enough evidence to believe the cell’s visible contents represent the settled canvas state rather than transient cursor or motion material.
A cell is contaminated when recent movement evidence indicates that the cursor or active visual object may currently occupy that cell.

A cell may be cleared of contamination when movement evidence shows that movement has moved elsewhere on the screen. For the current default design, movement elsewhere is treated as proof that the previously contaminated cell is now clean, because normal recordings are assumed to contain only one cursor-driven moving object.

A cell may also be cleared of contamination when a trustworthy local reference window shows the cell with no movement touching it and movement evidence indicates the cursor/active object is not parked in that cell. A no-movement window is not enough by itself if the most recent movement evidence suggests the cursor may have stopped inside the cell.
If a cell is relevant but not judgeable, it should be classified as unresolved rather than changed or unchanged.

For the current default design, Stage 4 may assume that movement elsewhere proves the evaluated cell is clean. If future benchmark footage shows UI animation, canvas movement, or other non-cursor motion causing false clean-state assumptions, this rule should be revisited.
Baseline Update Rule
Stage 4 begins each surviving union with the best trusted comparison state available from Stage 3.
For each relevant cell, this inherited Stage 3 state becomes the cell’s initial current_known_state.
When a probe confirms that a cell has changed, that probe receives positive changed-cell evidence.

A confirmed changed cell should update its current_known_state only when the changed state is visible in a trustworthy settled state, meaning Stage 4 can read the cell without cursor contamination or active movement interference.

Once a cell’s current_known_state has been updated to the changed state, later probes should not continue counting that same settled difference as new positive activity. The changed state becomes the new comparison state for that cell.
A confirmed unchanged cell does not create positive evidence. However, confirmed unchanged observations may refresh confidence that the current_known_state is still valid.

If a cell remains unresolved across multiple probes and is later resolved as confirmed_changed, Stage 4 should determine whether that change belongs to the earlier unresolved chain or only to the later probe. This attribution should follow the Backfill And Attribution Rule.

Global or broad canvas events, such as clear canvas, undo, transform, or move-canvas operations, may require broader current_known_state updates across many cells. The initial implementation may handle these through the same cell-level mechanism, but the design should allow later special handling if benchmark testing shows that broad events need separate treatment.
Change Credit Before Baseline Update Rule
A confirmed changed cell should not generate unlimited fresh positive credit across repeated probes before its current_known_state is updated.
When a cell is first confirmed_changed relative to its current_known_state, that change should create positive probe evidence for that probe.

After that first confirmed change, the same unresolved or not-yet-settled change may continue to support band continuity, but it should not be repeatedly counted as a brand-new changed event at every later probe unless Stage 4 has evidence of an additional distinct change beyond the already-detected one.
In other words:
- first confirmed change creates positive change credit
- continued visibility of that same unresolved change may support holding, continuation, or settlement tracking
- but it should not keep reopening or artificially strengthening probes as though a new change happened each time
A cell may generate new positive change credit later only if:
- its current_known_state has already been updated to a newer settled state and a further distinct change occurs after that
- or Stage 4 has strong evidence that a separate later change event has occurred in that same cell rather than the system merely continuing to observe the earlier change
This rule prevents one lasting update from being counted over and over again while the probe sequence waits for a clean settled-state baseline update.
Probe Classification Rule
Each Stage 4 probe should summarize its cell-level evidence into one final probe label.
The allowed probe labels are:
positive:

The probe has at least one judgeable cell that is confirmed_changed relative to that cell’s current_known_state. The threshold for confirmed_changed should be above normal flicker/noise, but low enough that small real art changes can still count.
negative:

The probe has no confirmed_changed cells, has enough judgeable relevant cells to support a meaningful decision, and has low unresolved support. A negative probe means Stage 4 has enough evidence to say that meaningful lasting canvas change is not currently present in the relevant cells.
holding:

The probe has no confirmed_changed cells at the current moment, but has unresolved evidence that is plausibly tied to ongoing valid activity or to a valid band that is already open. Holding means “do not close too hastily; this may still resolve into valid activity.”
unclear:

The probe does not have enough reliable evidence to open a band, continue a band, or close a band. Unclear is weaker than holding. It records insufficient evidence without strongly implying ongoing valid activity.
Initial default negative rule:
A probe may be labeled negative when:
- confirmed_changed_cells = 0
- judgeable_cells covers a meaningful portion of the currently relevant cells
- unresolved support is low enough that unresolved evidence is unlikely to hide meaningful valid activity

The exact numeric thresholds for “meaningful portion” and “low unresolved support” are tuning parameters and should be defined separately. Until benchmark testing provides stronger values, the implementation should prefer conservative negative decisions only when the relevant cells are mostly judgeable or when unresolved cells have no credible continuity with valid activity.
Initial default holding rule:
A probe may be labeled holding when:
- confirmed_changed_cells = 0
- unresolved cells remain relevant
- those unresolved cells are connected to recent activity, contamination, or an already-open valid activity band
- the evidence is not strong enough to label the probe negative
Initial default unclear rule:
A probe should be labeled unclear when:
- confirmed_changed_cells = 0
- the probe does not satisfy the negative rule
- the probe does not have enough unresolved continuity to satisfy the holding rule
Initial default operational thresholds
Until benchmark testing justifies better values, Stage 4 should use the following starting defaults:
- positive:
- negative:
confirmed_changed_cells = 0
and judgeable_cells covers at least 50 percent of the currently relevant cells
and unresolved_cells covers no more than 25 percent of the currently relevant cells
- holding:
confirmed_changed_cells = 0
and unresolved_cells covers more than 25 percent of the currently relevant cells
and the unresolved cells are continuous with recent activity or with an already-open band
- unclear:
used when the probe does not satisfy the positive rule, does not satisfy the negative rule, and does not have enough continuity to satisfy the holding rule
These are starting defaults, not final truths, and may be tuned later through benchmark testing.
Design caution:

Positive probes should be easier to prove than negative probes. A single clearly confirmed changed cell may be enough to justify a positive probe, while a negative probe should require broad confidence that unresolved cells are too few and too weak to plausibly hide meaningful valid activity.
Probe-Band Definition
A probe-band is a provisional time span inside a surviving union that Stage 4 believes contains meaningful valid art-changing activity.
A probe-band is built from a sequence of Stage 4 probe labels and cell-level evidence.
A probe-band is not a final clip. It is a Stage 4 structure that records where valid activity appears to begin, continue, and end at coarse probe resolution.
A probe-band may include positive probes and, in some cases, holding probes that are part of the same unresolved chain of activity.
A probe-band should not be created from holding or unclear probes alone. A probe-band requires positive changed-cell evidence somewhere in the band.
Stage 5 may later refine the start and end boundaries of a probe-band, especially when those boundaries were created from unresolved, holding, unclear, or backfilled evidence.
Band State Machine Rule
Stage 4 should build probe-bands using an explicit state machine.
The band state machine has two main states:
closed:
No valid activity band is currently open.
open:
A provisional valid activity band is currently active.
Opening rule:
A probe-band may open only when Stage 4 encounters a positive probe.
Holding probes may not open a probe-band by themselves.
Unclear probes may not open a probe-band by themselves.
Backfilled opening rule:

If a positive probe resolves an earlier continuous unresolved chain, Stage 4 may move the probe-band start earlier than the positive probe. This may happen only under the Backfill And Attribution Rule.
Continuation rule:
A positive probe continues an open probe-band.
A holding probe may continue an open probe-band when its unresolved cells are connected to recent activity in that band or are part of an unresolved chain that began inside that band.
An unclear probe should not strongly continue a band by itself. It may be carried as low-confidence interior uncertainty only if neighboring probes support the same band.
Closing rule:
A negative probe closes an open probe-band when the negative evidence is sufficiently resolved for the relevant cells.
A holding probe should not close a band.
An unclear probe should not close a band by itself.

If one or more unclear probes appear after the last positive or holding support, and no later probe restores changed support or credible holding continuity, the band should close at the last supported point before the unclear region.
The unclear region should then be marked as a low-confidence boundary zone for possible Stage 5 refinement rather than being silently absorbed into the band.
Reopening rule:
After a band closes, a new probe-band may open only on a later positive probe or on a later positive probe that backfills across a valid unresolved chain.
Internal uncertainty rule:

Holding or unclear probes may exist inside a probe-band only when surrounding evidence or later resolution supports that they belong to the same valid activity episode. If they do not resolve into support, they should become trim candidates for Stage 5 or be excluded from the final probe-band.
Backfill and Attribution Rule
Backfill is the process of attributing later resolved evidence to earlier unresolved activity.
Backfill exists because a cell may be active or contaminated during an earlier probe and only become readable in a later probe.
A later confirmed_changed result may backfill an earlier probe only when the earlier probe began a continuous unresolved chain that plausibly refers to the same local activity.
Stage 4 should distinguish between:
- late cell resolution
- band-boundary backfill
Late cell resolution means that a later probe finally resolves what happened to a previously unresolved cell.
Band-boundary backfill means that Stage 4 moves the start of a probe-band earlier in time based on that later resolution.
A cell may be resolved late without automatically forcing the probe-band start all the way back to the first unresolved probe.
A continuous unresolved chain may be considered valid for backfill when:
- the same cell, or a small connected local group of cells, remains unresolved or relevant across consecutive probes
- the chain is not broken by a strong negative probe for that same local cell group
- the chain does not jump across unrelated activity elsewhere in the union
- the later confirmed change occurs close enough in time to plausibly resolve the earlier unresolved activity
Initial default backfill limit:
Later evidence may resolve the history of an earlier unresolved cell across a long span of time if the unresolved chain remains continuous and local.
However, probe-band boundary backfill should be much more conservative.

For the current default design, Stage 4 should move a probe-band start earlier by no more than 1 or 2 probe intervals unless the unresolved chain remains continuous, local, and unbroken by any strong negative probe.

If later evidence resolves a cell but the unresolved chain is too long or too weak to justify safe band-boundary backfill, Stage 4 should keep the earlier boundary as low confidence and hand that uncertainty to Stage 5.
End-Of-Union Late Resolution Rule
Stage 4 must define whether very late evidence just after the end of a surviving union may be used to resolve cells that are still unresolved at the union boundary.
For the current default design, Stage 4 may use a short post-union late-resolution allowance only for resolving the final local state of cells that were already relevant before the union ended.

This allowance must be narrow and local. Its purpose is not to borrow unrelated future evidence, but to let the system read cells whose final settled state becomes visible only a few frames after the union boundary.
Initial default rule:
- Stage 4 may inspect a short post-union resolution window immediately after union end
- this window should be used only for cells already present in the final unresolved chain near the union boundary
- it must not be used to discover new later activity outside that unresolved boundary problem
- it must not borrow evidence from a separate later activity episode

If the final unresolved cells cannot be safely resolved within this short post-union allowance, the boundary should remain low confidence and should be handed to Stage 5 rather than forced into a confident Stage 4 decision.
Boundary Confidence Handoff Rule
Stage 4 should attach boundary confidence metadata to each probe-band.
Boundary confidence describes how certain Stage 4 is about the probe-band’s start and end times.
High-confidence start:
A band start is high confidence when the band opens directly on a positive probe with clear confirmed_changed cells and little unresolved ambiguity at the opening edge.
Low-confidence start:
A band start is low confidence when it depends on backfilled unresolved evidence, holding probes, unclear probes, contaminated cells, or weak changed support.
High-confidence end:
A band end is high confidence when the band closes on a sufficiently resolved negative probe with low unresolved support.
Low-confidence end:
A band end is low confidence when the band closes after holding or unclear probes, when unresolved cells remain near the edge, or when the closing evidence is weak.
Stage 5 should focus on low-confidence starts and low-confidence ends.
Stage 5 should not re-analyze the entire interior of a high-confidence probe-band unless later evidence shows that the Stage 4 structure is wrong.
Placeholder tuning guidance
The exact numeric parameters for Stage 4 are still tuning parameters, not final design truths.
These include:
- the coarse probe interval
- the probe-local evaluation window length
- the changed-cell threshold above noise
- the unchanged-cell threshold
- the unresolved band width
- the minimum changed support needed for a positive probe
- the unchanged support needed for a negative probe
- the holding-probe rule
- the provisional band opening rule
- the provisional band closing rule
These values should be defined separately as tunable parameters, not embedded into the conceptual definition itself.
Open parameter questions
The exact placeholder values still need to be chosen for:
- coarse probe interval
- probe-local evaluation window length
- changed-cell threshold
- unchanged-cell threshold
- unresolved band width
- positive probe threshold
- negative probe threshold
- holding-probe rule
- valid band opening rule
- valid band closing rule
These are tuning parameters, not conceptual definitions.
Stage 5: Local Boundary Refinement
Purpose
Stage 5 refines uncertain boundaries left behind by Stage 4.
Its role is no longer to serve as the primary discovery engine for where valid activity exists inside a surviving union. Stage 4 should already have identified the provisional valid activity bands.
Stage 5 exists to improve temporal precision at the edges of those provisional bands when the coarse Stage 4 probe scan was structurally correct but not yet frame-accurate enough.
Inputs

text:list-item.text:p: - provisional valid activity bands from Stage 4
- uncertain or low-confidence Stage 4 band boundaries
- movement evidence and reusable sampled-frame data from earlier stages
- Stage 4 probe evidence and boundary metadata

office:text.text:p: Outputs

text:list-header.text:p: Stage 5 outputs a refined list of retained activity bands and boundary-adjusted supporting material.

text:list-item.text:p: - start_time
- end_time
- parent_union_index
- classification
- whether the unit came from inward trimming, outward recovery, or unchanged carry-through
- boundary confidence metadata

text:list-header.text:p: Stage 5 does not create a new broad structural interpretation of the surviving union. Its outputs are refinements of the provisional valid activity bands already identified by Stage 4.

office:text.text:p: Responsibilities

text:list-item.text:p: - refines uncertain starts and ends of Stage 4 provisional bands
- performs more local and more precise checks near those boundaries
- trims over-kept idle material from valid bands
- recovers edge material that Stage 4 could not confidently keep or reject
- resolves small uncertain boundary zones into final valid, boundary, or invalid material
Non-Responsibilities
Stage 5 does not:
- rediscover the broad internal structure of a surviving union from scratch
- act as the default mechanism for finding valid bands that Stage 4 failed to identify
- repeatedly subdivide already-clear valid interiors without boundary uncertainty

office:text.text:p: Evidence reuse rule
Stage 5 refines the movement evidence and slice metadata carried forward from earlier stages rather than re-scanning raw video from scratch by default.
Stage 5 Boundary Refinement Scope
Stage 5 should focus only on local boundary refinement problems.
Examples include:
- a valid Stage 4 band that begins slightly too early
- a valid Stage 4 band that ends slightly too late
- a short uncertain bridge between two clearly valid regions
- a narrow unresolved edge where contamination prevented a clean Stage 4 decision
Stage 5 should therefore operate on limited edge regions rather than broad whole-band interiors whenever possible.
Design principles for Stage 5

text:list-item.text:p: - Stage 5 refines boundaries rather than discovering broad activity structure
- it should operate locally and conservatively
- it should spend computation mainly where Stage 4 left genuine uncertainty
- it should avoid re-analyzing already-clear valid interiors unnecessarily
- it should improve temporal precision without undoing Stage 4’s broader structural decisions unless strong evidence requires that

office:text.text:p: Placeholder Tuning Guidance
The exact numeric parameters for Stage 5 are still tuning parameters, not final design truths.
These include:
- the boundary refinement window length
- the fine boundary probe interval
- the maximum distance Stage 5 is allowed to trim inward from a Stage 4 provisional boundary in one pass
- the maximum distance Stage 5 is allowed to extend outward when recovering edge material
- the evidence threshold for confirming a refined boundary
- the unresolved tolerance allowed at a refined boundary
These values should be defined separately as tunable parameters, not embedded into the conceptual definition itself.
Open Parameter Questions
The exact placeholder values still need to be chosen for:
- boundary refinement window length
- fine boundary probe interval
- inward trim limit
- outward recovery limit
- refined boundary evidence threshold
- unresolved tolerance at a refined boundary
These are tuning parameters, not conceptual definitions.
Stage 6: Final Clip Construction
Purpose
Stage 6 takes the retained material produced by Stage 4 and Stage 5 and constructs the final clips.
Its role is to:
- gather all retained units
- merge nearby retained units into pre-clips
- apply lead-in and tail-after to each pre-clip
- merge any expanded clips that overlap or touch
- output the final clip ranges
Stage 6 is the first stage that creates output intended for export or editing use.
Inputs

text:list-item.text:p: - refined valid activity bands
- refined boundary-supporting retained material
Together, these form the retained material used by Stage 6.

office:text.text:p: Outputs
Stage 6 outputs a list of final clips.
Each clip stores:
- start_time
- end_time
Responsibilities
Stage 6:
- collects all retained units
- orders them chronologically
- merges nearby retained units into pre-clips using the merge rule
- ensures each pre-clip contains at least one valid retained band and is not formed from boundary-supporting retained material alone
- applies lead-in and tail-after to each pre-clip
- merges any expanded clips that overlap or touch
- outputs the final clip list
Non-responsibilities
Stage 6 does not:
- classify material as valid, invalid, unresolved, or boundary-supporting
- refine unresolved material further
- revisit earlier union or band-level decisions
Pre-clip rule
A pre-clip is formed by merging nearby retained units that are close enough in time to belong together under the merge rule.
A pre-clip must contain at least one valid retained band.
Boundary-supporting retained material may contribute to a pre-clip, but may not form a pre-clip on its own.
Merge rule
Two retained units may merge into the same pre-clip when:

text:list-item.text:p: they overlap in time
or they touch
or the gap between them is no greater than the maximum merge gap

office:text.text:p: The maximum merge gap should be small and conservative, because boundary material already provides a buffer around valid material.
Union-lineage rule
Stage 6 is allowed to merge retained units across original surviving union boundaries when the normal merge rule is satisfied.
Candidate unions and surviving unions are analysis containers, not final clip boundaries.

A final clip may therefore include retained material that originated from more than one surviving union, provided the merged result still contains at least one valid retained band and does not violate the normal merge constraints.
Lead-in and tail-after rule
Lead-in and tail-after are applied after retained units have already been merged into a pre-clip.
They are not used to decide whether retained units should merge.
This keeps the merge logic structural and the lead-in / tail-after logic editorial.
Post-expansion merge rule
If lead-in and tail-after expansion causes two clips to overlap or touch, they are merged into a single final clip.
This avoids duplicated overlapping footage and keeps the final output clean.
Testing rule
During testing and validation, lead-in and tail-after may be set to zero so that the underlying merge and refinement behavior can be inspected without editorial expansion masking errors.
Design principles for Stage 6
Stage 6 should follow these principles:
- Stage 6 performs construction, not classification
- every final clip must contain at least one valid retained band and may not be formed from boundary-supporting retained material alone
- boundary-supporting retained material may support a clip, but may not create one on its own
- merge decisions should be conservative
- lead-in and tail-after should be applied after pre-clips are formed
- overlapping expanded clips should merge into one final clip
Placeholder tuning guidance
The exact numeric parameters for Stage 6 are still tuning parameters, not final design truths.
These include:

text:list-item.text:p: the maximum merge gap
the lead-in duration
the tail-after duration

office:text.text:p: These values should be defined separately as tunable parameters, not embedded into the conceptual definition itself.
Implementation note: Final clip boundaries should not extend beyond the actual start or end of the source footage.
Open parameter questions
The exact placeholder values still need to be chosen for:

text:list-item.text:p: maximum merge gap
lead-in duration
tail-after duration

office:text.text:p: These are tuning parameters, not conceptual definitions.
